funder.orgName: National Natural Science Foundation of China

affiliation.orgName: Laboratory of Control of Power Transmission and Conversion of Ministry of Education
School of Electronic Information and Electrical Engineering
Norwegian University of Science and Technology

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Loop gain-based eigen-sensitivity (LGES) is a useful frequency-domain tool for oscillation diagnosis of converter-based system. However, the existing theory is still scant in two aspects: participation factor (PF) is bound up with the frequency-domain modal characteristic that does not necessarily point to the stability as that of the time-domain eigen-sensitivity (i.e., PF of oscillation mode); a systematic LGES analysis framework containing both component-and parameter-level sensitivity is missing. These two factors hinder the application of LGES method on the proper evaluation of stability effects, which are closely related with the time-domain oscillation mode. To address these issues, this paper proposes a multilayer LGES method directed to the oscillation mode, and a full set of indices like PF, component and parameter sensitivity are established. The link from the eigen-sensitivity of frequency domain to that of time domain is revealed, through which it is shown how the proposed LGES method can facilitate the control parameter tuning-guided oscillation suppression. The effectiveness of the proposed LGES method is validated via case studies conducted on a generic AC/DC converter-based system.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: HE demand of low-carbon energy utilization stimulates the fast development of converters-interfaced renewable power generations (RPGs) like wind power, and converters-based grid interconnectors such as high-voltage dc (HVDC) transmission. This leads to a paradigm shift of traditional power system to a more converter-dominated power system [1]. Interactions among converter controls and electric networks result in a series of oscillation incidents, which are continually reported recently [2]. Thus, oscillatory stability issues of converter-based systems are becoming a very active research topic at present.

In this context, the impedance-based analysis method for the oscillatory stability analysis is widely applied [3], due to its superior traits of compactness and modular scalability. Earlystage researches are more focused on the mechanism analysis of those control-induced oscillation issues, and thus a single machine system [4], [5] (e.g., a single grid-tied converter) is usually adopted. As the advancement of research, current focus of analysis is gradually shifted to the multi-machine system [6], [7] (e.g., a multi-converters based system), concentrating more on oscillation diagnostics like oscillation source identification, propagation, attenuation, etc. This shift also greatly promotes the development of the impedance-based analysis method from the classical single-source-load method [3] (i.e., converter and grid respectively as the load and source subsystem) to the multisource-load one [8], [9] (i.e., various converters and grids as a group of load and source subsystems). And for the latter case, both the source and load subsystems are modeled in the network form like impedance network or admittance network [9].

Given that the impedance/admittance network retains the structural information of the overall system [8], the networkbased analysis has great potential of revealing multi-machine interactive behaviors and pinpointing crucial instability factors, through which a full view of the oscillation generation and propagation can be acquired [10]. In contrast, it is difficult to obtain such information via the (aggregated) single-source-load method, because the system structural information is lost by impedance aggregation [9]. One step further, to expose the structural information (e.g., nodes, infrastructures, etc.) in oscillation diagnostics, the eigen-sensitivity using the networkbased frequency-domain model is developed. In this regard, depending on which type of frequency domain model is adopted, there are two representative ways [11]- [17]:

The Nodal Admittance-based Eigen-Sensitivity (NAES): This method originates from the resonance modal analysis [11], [12] of passive transmission networks, and has been extended to the converter-based AC or AC/DC system [10], [13]- [15] recently. The main idea is to establish the system node admittance model Y s in frequency domain, where passive elements and converters are modelled by two-port ac or three-port ac/dc admittances [13], [15]. Then, the oscillation mode is identified by calculating right-half-plane (RHP) zeros of det Y s , based on which the eigenvalue decomposition is conducted to obtain the node sensitivity (referred to as the participation factor (PF)). And the derivative chain rule can be further applied to establish the component-level sensitivity beneath the PF and its next level sensitivity, i.e., parameter sensitivity [12]- [14]. Since T the above sensitivity indices are all with respect to the modal admittance, [16] also proposes a transformation method to connect them with the time-domain oscillation mode, so as to facilitate the controller tuning and oscillation suppression.

div.head: The Loop Gain-based Eigen-Sensitivity (LGES):
div.p: This method is inspired by the way of performing the generalized Nyquist criterion (GNC)-based stability test, for which a socalled generalized source-load model [17] is proposed. The whole system will be split into two subnetworks, one is the source subsystem with all passive elements and the other is the load subsystem with all converters. Then, the system loop gain L s with multi-ports can be obtained, with which the same eigenvalue decomposition is applied to acquire loop gain-based PFs [18]. Compared with the NAES method, the existing LGES method is still scant in two aspects: a) the existing loop gainbased PF is a frequency-domain modal characteristic [18] that does not necessarily point to the stability as that of the timedomain eigen-sensitivity (i.e., PF of oscillation mode); b) a systematic analysis framework of LGES including the component and parameter -level sensitivity are missing.
To bridge the above gap and aimed for developing a multilayer LGES framework as the counterpart to the NAES method, this paper will contribute to the following aspects:

1) Propose a multilayer LGES method with respect to the time-domain oscillation mode, and indices like PF, component and parameter sensitivity are all established; 2) The link from eigen-sensitivity of frequency domain to that of time domain is revealed, through which it is shown how the proposed LGES method can facilitate the parameter tuning-based oscillation suppression. The rest of paper is arranged as follows: Section II makes a review on the existing LGES method. Section III introduces the proposed multilayer LGES method. Section IV validates the correctness of the proposed LGES method. Section V shows the application of the proposed LGES method on the oscillation suppression. Section VI concludes the paper.

div.head: II. INTRODUCTION OF EXISTING LGES ANALYSIS METHOD
div.p: This Section will briefly review the loop gain model and the existing calculation method of its eigen-sensitivity, whose parts can be improved are clearly pointed out.

div.head: A. Loop Gain Model of Network-based Converter System
div.p: Recently, a frequency-domain loop gain model is proposed to characterize small-signal dynamics of multi-converters system in the network form, with the advantage of being immune to those nasty RHP-poles [15], [17]. As intuitively shown in Fig. 1, the whole system is split at the nodes (denoted as red circles) connected with converters. Then, generalized source and load subsystems [17] with multi-ports can be established, and the resulting closed-loop model is like:
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) net node node net con s s s s s = ⋅ + Z V I I Z Y (1
div.p: where I node s and V node s are node currents and voltages, respectively. Z net s denotes the node impedance of the source subsystem with only passive elements, where internal nodes (denoted as blue circles) are eliminated via the Kron reduction. Y con s denotes the node admittance of the load subsystem containing only converters. I is the identity matrix. Since Z net s is constituted of pure passive elements, it is free of RHP poles and zeros. Then, the stability of closed-loop system in (1) can be determined by its loop gain L s .

div.formula: net con s s s = + L I Z Y (2
div.p: The system is stable if and only if there exists no RHP zero in det L s . Supposing an unstable oscillation at s= 0 happens, i.e., an RHP zero appears, it should fulfill:
div.formula: ( ) 0 det 0 l é ù = ë û L (3)
div.p: To simplify notation,  0 in brackets will be omitted hereinafter, i.e., L  0 will be denoted as the notation L.

div.head: B. Loop Gain-based Participation Factor
div.p: The eigen-sensitivity analysis method [18] is also developed for such loop gain model, mainly the participation factor (PF), so as to facilitate the oscillation source locating. Specifically, the eigen-decomposition will be applied to L as:
div.formula: 1 1 2 2 1 2 = L L L L L L L L L L n L L n n ⋅ ⋅ é ùé ù L ê úê ú ê úê ú ê úê ú L ê úê ú é ù = ⋅ ⋅ ê úê ú ê ú ë û ê úê ú ê úê ú ê úê ú L ê úê ú ë ûë û    L R T t t r r r t L(4)
div.p: where  i L is defined as the modal loop gain; R L is the right column eigenvector, T L is the left row eigenvector and it has R L T L =I. The superscript 'L' denotes that variables belong to the loop gain-based eigen-sensitivity (LGES), so as to distinguish from those variables of the node admittance-based eigen-sensitivity (NAES) [13] .
The eigenvalue matrix  L can be represented as:
div.formula: L L L = ⋅ ⋅ T L R L(5)
div.p: There will appear an eigenvalue  i L in  L close to 0, which corresponds to that one Nyquist loci will cross the -1,j0 . This eigenvalue is defined as the critical modal loop gain. Supposing  1 L 0 (i.e., critical modal loop gain), it can be expressed as in (6) according to (5).
-Fig. 1. Generalized source-load partition and the resulting closed-loop model.
div.formula: 1 1 1 L L L L = ⋅ ⋅ t L r (6)
div.p: where t 1 L and r 1 L are the corresponding critical eigenvectors. Based on (6), loop gain-based PF (referred as LG-PF hereinafter) is established in [18] as:
div.formula: 1 1 1 11 L L L LL L ji j i ij ij L L ¶L ¶ é ù é ù = ⋅ ⋅ = = ê ú ê ú ë û ë û ¶ ¶ L t r r t P F (7)
div.p: where PF L is defined as the LG-PF matrix.

It can be seen that the existing LG-PF reflects the sensitivity of system parts L ij to the critical modal loop gain  1 L , which is in frequency domain. However, in terms of stability effects, the sensitivity of L ij to the time-domain oscillation mode  0 is more concerned. This is because the oscillation diagnosis and suppression are realized mainly through the movement of oscillation mode  0 from the unstable RHP to the stable lefthalf-plane (LHP). Also, the specific suppression strategy is conducted from the level of components and parameters, while their sensitivities are still absent.

div.head: III. A MULTILAYER LGES ANALYSIS METHOD WITH RESPECT
div.p: TO THE OSCILLATION MODE To address above issues, this Section will introduce a multilayer LGES analysis method. Three sensitivity indices, including loop PF, component-and parameter-sensitivity, are established and all linked to the oscillation mode.

div.head: A. Outline of Multilayer LGES Index System
div.p: The oscillation mode  0 , i.e., the RHP zero of det L s , is exactly the eigenvalue [14] of the state-space matrix A as shown in (8). In this sense, the oscillation mode  0 mainly reflects the system time-domain characteristics.
div.formula: ( ) ( ) { } 0 eig det 0 s l é ù Î  = ë û A L (8
div.p: The critical modal loop gain  1 L is the eigenvalue of the frequency-domain loop gain matrix L as shown in (9). It mainly reflects the system frequency-domain characteristics.
div.formula: ( ) { } 1 eig L L Î L (9)
div.p: The establishment procedure of the multilayer eigensensitivity is presented in Fig. 2. The first step is to bridge the gap between the critical modal loop gain  1 L and the oscillation mode  0 , which will be derived concretely in Section B. With this relation, the existing PF of the critical modal loop gain can be transformed into that of the oscillation mode. Then, based on the derivative chain rule, the oscillation mode-directed component-and parameter-sensitivity can be computed, which will be presented carefully in Section C.

div.head: B. LG-PF of Oscillation Mode
div.p: As stated above, the connection between two kinds of PFs (i.e.,
div.formula:  1 L L ij and  0 L ij
div.p: ) will be derived first. Based on the derivative chain rule, det L is utilized as a bridge to connect
div.formula:  1 L L ij with  0 L ij . 0 0 1 1 det det L L ij ij L L l l é ù ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ L ë û = ⋅ ⋅ é ù ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶L ë û L L (10)
div.p: Then, two partial derivatives concerning det L needs to be calculated. Since there exists an RHP zero  0 (i.e., oscillation mode), det L s can be represented in the pole-zero form as:
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) 0 0 det L i i i g s s s s s p l l ¹ - é ù = - ⋅ ë û -    L (11
div.p: The partial derivative of  0 to det L s can be obtained as:
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) 0 det L s g s l é ù ¶ =- ¶ ⋅ ë û L (12
div.p: By substituting  0 into g L s , it has:
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) 0 1 0 0 det det L s g s l l l - = ¶ ¶ = = - é ù é ù ¶ ¶ ë û ë û L L (13
div.p: where the notation g L represents the g L  0 .
Since the eigen-matrix has been derived in ( 4), det L s can also be represented by the product of all eigenvalues as:
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) ( ) 1 1 det n L L i i s s s ¹ = L ⋅ L  L (14
div.p: Similar to (11)~( 13), the partial derivative of det L to  1 L can be obtained as in (15).
div.formula: ( ) ( ) ( ) 0 1 1 1 det det n L i L L i s s s l ¹ = é ù é ù ¶ ¶ ë û ë û = = L ¶L ¶L  L L (15
div.p: where the notation
div.formula:  i L represents the  i L  0 .
div.p: By substituting ( 13) and ( 15) into (10), the following relation can be acquired.
div.formula: 1 0 1 1 L n L L L L L i ji i ij ij g L L x l x - ¹ ¶ ¶ L é ù = - L ⋅ = ⋅ ê ú ë û ¶ ¶   PF (16
div.p: where  L is a complex-valued modification factor.
Based on ( 16), the LG-PF matrix with respect to the oscillation mode (denoted as PF mod L ) can be expressed as:
div.formula: mod L L L x = ⋅ PF PF (17

div.head: C. Component and Parameter Sensitivity of Oscillation Mode 1) Components and Parameters Inside Z net
div.p: The sensitivity of impedance component (e.g., Z net,ij ) with respect to the oscillation mode can be calculated from that with respect to the critical modal loop gain.
div.formula: 0 0 1 1 net, net, net, 1 det det L L L L ij ij ij Z Z Z l l x é ù ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ L ¶ L ë û = ⋅ ⋅ = ⋅ é ù ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶L ë û L L (18
div.p: By substituting ( 6) into (18), it has ( )
div.formula: 1 n e t c o n 1 0 1 net, net, net, net con 1 c o n n e t 1 net, net, net, L L L L L ij ij ij L L L ij ij ij Z Z Z Z Z Z l x x x é ù ¶ + ¶ ¶ L ë û = ⋅ = ⋅ ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ = ⋅ + ¶ ¶ ae ö ÷ ç ÷ ç + ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç è ø ¶ t I Z Y r Z Y t I Y Z r(19)
div.p: I and Y con do not contain any element concerning Z net,ij , meaning that their corresponding partial derivatives equal to zero. Then, (19) can be simplified as:
div.formula: 0 n e t 1 c o n 1 net, net, 1 c o n1 con, 1 11 con, 1 con, 1 co 1 1 1 n, , , , 0 0 0 , 1 0 ij L L L L i i L L L L L ij ij L L L L j j nn L j j i n i n Z Z Y r Y r Y Y t t PF PF l x x x x ae ö ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç è ø ae é ù ö ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ê ú ç ÷ = ç ÷ ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç è ø ë ¶ ¶ = ⋅ ¶ ¶ ⋅ ⋅ + + é ⋅ ⋅ û = ⋅ ⋅ ⋅ ù = ⋅ ë û        Z t Y r t Y r T con L ji L x é ù ê ú ë û é ù = ⋅ ê ú ë û P Y F (20)
div.p: By substituting ( 17) into (20), it can be obtained as:
div.formula: 0 con net, mod ji L ij Z l ¶ é ù = ê ú ë û ¶ Y PF (21)
div.p: Based on the derivative chain rule, the parameter sensitivity can be further derived from the above component sensitivity.
Supposing parameter  z affects some impedance components Z net,ij , its sensitivity can be calculated as in (22).
div.formula: net, net, 0 0 con net d , mo ij ij ji i L z j z z Z Z Z l l r r r = ae ö ae ö ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ ÷ ÷ ç ç é ù ÷ ÷ ç ç ÷ ÷ ç ç ê ú ÷ ÷ ë û ç ç ÷ ÷ ç ç ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ è ø è ø = å å PF Y (22)
div.p: where  represents that all components containing  z should be considered in the form of summation. The subscript ' z ' represents parameters of passive elements in Z net s .

div.head: 2) Components and Parameters Inside Y con
div.p: The admittance component sensitivity can be calculated in a similar way as (20), where Y con,ij is taken for derivation. At this time, I and Z net will not contain any element concerning Y con,ij , ( 19) can be rewritten as:
div.formula: 0 c o n 1 n e t 1 con, con, 1 n e t 1 net,1 1 net, 1 net,1 net, 11 1 1 0 0 1 0 0 , , , , ij L L L L n L L j j n L L L ij ij L L L L i j ni j L i Y Y Z r P Z r Z t t PF Z F l x x x x ae ö ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç ÷ ç è ø ae é ùö ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ê ú ç ÷ ç ÷ ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ç è ø ë û = ⋅ ⋅ ⋅ ¶ ¶ = ⋅ ¶ ¶ = ⋅ ⋅ + + é ù = ⋅ ⋅ ê ú ë û ⋅        Y t Z r t Z r n m o T net d et ni L ji ji L L x é ù ë û é ù é ù = ⋅ = ê ú ê ú ë û û ⋅ ë Z PF PF Z (23)
div.p: Supposing  y affects some admittance components Y con,ij , its sensitivity can be calculated similarly as in (24).
div.formula: con, con o , 0 0 net con, m d = ij ij ji y i j y y L Y Y Y l l r r r = ae ö ae ö ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ ÷ ÷ ç ç é ù ÷ ÷ ç ç ÷ ÷ ç ç ê ú ÷ ÷ ë û ç ç ÷ ÷ ç ç ¶ ¶ ¶ ¶ è ø è ø å å Z PF (24)
div.p: where subscript 'y' denotes converters' parameters in Y con s .

So far, the multilayer eigen-sensitivity of the loop gain L s has been established, and main sensitivity indices are summarized in Table I. These indices are all complex-valued involving information of both magnitude and phase to the oscillation mode, which can greatly facilitate the oscillation suppression. This will be explained carefully in Section V.

div.head: IV. CASE STUDIES AND VALIDATION
div.p: This Section will implement some case studies on a typical AC/DC converter-dominated system, based on which the correctness of established multilayer LGES indices is verified.

div.head: A. System Description
div.p: A three-terminal HVDC system shown in Fig. 3 is selected as the test case. Small-signal dynamics of the converter are modelled by a three-port ac/dc admittance model [15]. The model of the load subsystem Y con s is given in (25), and the source subsystem Z net s can be modelled in a similar way (more details can be referred to [17] and [19]).

div.formula: con con1 con2 con3 , , s s s blkdiag s é ù = ë û Y Y Y Y (25
div.p: Notations of each non-zero Z net or Y con component and their internal parameters are listed in Table II. For this system, a typical marginal unstable condition is considered, i.e., increasing the PLL bandwidth  PQ S1 of sending converter-2 (SEC-2). Fig. 4 presents the pole-zero distribution of this oscillation case, and there appears one pair of RHP zeros  1,2 0.0794j53.2 (i.e., oscillation mode). The rest of poles and zeros will be used for calculating (11).

div.head: B. Results of Multilayer LGES Analysis 1) Results of LG-PF
div.p: By substituting the obtained oscillation mode (e.g.,  1 0.0794j53.2) into L s , its eigenvalues can be calculated using (4) and results are listed in Table III. It can be seen that

div.head: TABLE I SUMMARIZATION OF PROPOSED LGES ANALYSIS METHOD
Eigen-Sensitivity Indices Calculation Formula
div.p: Participation Factor
div.formula: mo 0 d ji ij L L l ¶ é ù = ê ú ë û ¶ PF Component Sensitivity passive elements 0 con net, mod ji L ij Z l ¶ é ù = ê ú ë û ¶ Y PF converters m 0 net con, od ji j L i Y l ¶ é ù = ê ú ë û ¶ Z PF Parameter Sensitivity parameters of passive elements d net, 0 mo con ij ji z L z Z l r r ae ö ¶ ¶ ÷ ç é ù ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ¶ = ÷ ë û ç ÷ ç ¶ è ø å PF Y parameters of converters con, 0 net d mo i L ij j y y Y l r r ae ö ¶ ¶ ÷ ç é ù ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ¶ = ÷ ë û ç ÷ ç ¶ è ø å Z PF  2
div.p: L 0 is the critical modal loop gain, based on which r 2 L and t 2 L are extracted as critical eigenvectors. Then, according to ( 13), ( 15) and ( 16), both g L and ∏  i L 9 i2 are calculated to obtain the modification factor  L and results are given in Table III.

With the identified critical eigenvectors and modification factor, PF mod L can be obtained. To make data more readable, the trace of PF mod L is used as the per-unit base, and results of the normalized PF mod L are given in Fig. 5(a). It can be clearly seen that nodes #3d, #3q are with the largest values, meaning that interactions between AC Grid-2 and SEC-2 are dominant in this oscillation case.

div.head: ) Results of Component and Parameter Sensitivity
div.p: With the calculation of PF mod L , the component sensitivity concerning Z net and Y con can be obtained according to ( 21) and ( 23), whose results are given in Fig. 5

div.head: C. Validation
div.p: The above theoretical calculation results will be compared with actual tuning results, so as to justify their accuracies.

div.head: 1) Validation of LG-PF
div.p: The actual tuning results for the above PF and sensitivity indices will be calculated here. For example, a 5% increment in the real part of L ij is considered, and the predicted change in  0 can be calculated based on (26).
div.formula: 0 m o d Re 5% Y ij ji L l é ù D = ⋅ D ⋅ ê ú ë û PF (26
div.p: For comparison, the system  0 is re-computed for 5% increment in the real part of L ij and the actual change from the original condition is denoted as  0 ' . The error between the predicted  0 and actual  0 ' are calculated in Fig. 6(a), most of which are within 10%. The predictions are not perfect because steady-state operation points are assumed unchanged.

div.head: ) Validation of Component and Parameter Sensitivity
div.p: The same procedure is repeated for the error analysis of the component and parameter sensitivity. For example, a 5% increment in the real part of Z net,ij and  z is considered, and the predicted change  0 can be calculated based on ( 27) and (28).
This is also valid for Y con,ij and  y .
div.formula: o 0 c o n e d n t , m Re 5% L ij ji Z l é ù D = ⋅ D ⋅ ê ú ë û Y PF (27) ( ) d net, 0 c o n m o 5% L ij z ji z Z l r r ae ö ¶ ÷ ç é ù ÷ ç D = ⋅ D ⋅ ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ë û ç ÷ ç ¶ è ø å PF Y (28)
div.p: Similarly, the system  0 is re-computed for the above increment and the obtained actual  0 ' are compared with predicted  0 . The results of the corresponding error analysis are presented in Fig. 6(b),(c) and (e), where most errors are within 5%.
So far, the correctness of the proposed multilayer LGES analysis method has been validated, including indices of LG-PF, component-and parameter-level sensitivity.

div.head: V. APPLICATION ON THE OSCILLATION SUPPRESSION
div.p: This section will exhibit the potential of the proposed LGES method on the oscillation suppression, mainly the role of the parameter sensitivity played in guiding the controller tuning.

div.head: A. Parameter Sensitivity-Guided Controller Tuning
div.p: As shown in Fig. 5  cc S2 with high magnitude are regarded as dominant parameters in this oscillation case. Their parameter sensitivity given in Fig. 5 (b) and (c) are also depicted in the complex plane, as shown in Fig. 7(a). The length of the parameter sensitivity vector is exactly its magnitude, while the direction of the parameter sensitivity vector is namely its phase. If the parameter sensitivity vector is directed to the RHP unstable plane, the value of this parameter should be decreased, and vice versa. Such complex-plane characterization can be regarded as the traction action of parameters to the oscillation mode (e.g.,  1 in the above case study).

As a comparison, the parameter sensitivity with respect to the critical modal loop gain (i.e.,  2 L in this case) are also shown in Fig. 7(b). It can be obtained conveniently by replacing PF mod L in ( 22) and (24) with PF L , which is like:
div.formula: net, 2 con L ij ji z L z Z r r ae ö ¶ ¶L ÷ ç é ù ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ë û ç ÷ ç ¶ ¶ ø = è å F Y P (29) con, 2 net = L L ij ji y y Y r r ae ö ¶ ¶L ÷ ç é ù ÷ ç ÷ ç ê ú ÷ ë û ç ÷ ç ¶ ¶ è ø å Z PF(30)
div.p: Time-domain simulations are given in Fig. 7(c) to test which parameter sensitivity is more suitable for guiding the oscillation suppression. According to Fig. 7(a), the system will become more stable by decreasing L ac g2 , and the oscillation frequency should increase. This is validated in Fig. 7(c) that the system becomes stable when decreasing L ac g2 by 3%, and the oscillation frequency increases from 8.5 Hz to 8.625 Hz. However, Fig. 7(b) gives a contrary predication that the system will become more stable by increasing L ac g2 . Results of other parameters shown in Fig. 7(c) also conform with predictions in Fig. 7(a), for instance, the system becomes more stable if decreasing  cc S1 and  pll S2 , or increasing  cc S2 . But Fig. 7(b) will give misleading information, and thus the established parameter sensitivity with respect to the oscillation mode is more suitable for guiding the controller tuning-based oscillation suppression.

div.head: B. Discussion on the Reference for Determining Dominant Parameters
div.p: An interesting phenomenon can be observed by comparing Fig. 5(b), (c) with Fig. 7(c) that the magnitude of the parameter sensitivity may not accurately reflect the dominance of parameters on the stability margin. In Fig. 7(c), all parameter changes are under the same ratio, i.e., 3%, so as to compare suppression effects of each parameter fairly. It can be seen that suppression effects of  cc S2 and L ac g2 are the most obvious. This is reasonable for  cc S2 , whose sensitivity magnitude is the largest as 0.45 given in Fig. 5(c). However, the sensitivity magnitude of  cc S1 is greater than that of L ac g2 (0.18>0.14 shown in Fig. 5(c)), while suppression effect of  cc S1 is far weaker than L ac g2 . The reason behind is that the system stability margin is more related to the movement of the oscillation mode on the real axis, while the magnitude of the parameter sensitivity is a comprehensive evaluation for the influence of both real axis and imaginary axis. It can be seen in Fig. 7(a) that the sensitivity phase of  cc S1 is nearly 0.5 (almost perpendicular to the real axis), meaning that its influence imposed on the real axis is small despite of large magnitude.

The comparison on the magnitude of different parameter sensitivities is presented in Fig. 8. Para-Sen1 denotes the magnitude of the parameter sensitivity to the modal loop gain (i.e., traditional LGES) shown in (29) and (30). Para-Sen2 denotes the magnitude of the proposed parameter sensitivity to the oscillation mode, and its projection on real axis is denoted as Para-Sen3. The per unit transformation is applied to the Para-Sen1, Para-Sen2 and Para-Sen3. It can be seen that Para-Sen1 is the same as Para-Sen2, because constant  L will be offset in (31) According to Para-Sen3 in Fig. 8, the dominance order should be as:  cc S2 >L ac g2 > pll S2 > cc S1 , which fits well with timedomain simulations in Fig. 7(c). Therefore, the projection of the established parameter sensitivity on the real axis is more suitable as the reference for determining dominant parameters, in terms of the oscillation suppression.

div.head: VI. CONCLUSION
div.p: This paper proposes a multilayer LGES analysis method to facilitate the oscillation source locating and suppression of the converter-dominated system. Three crucial indices directed to the time-domain oscillation mode are established, including loop gain-based PF, component-and parameter-sensitivity, whose correctness is validated. Main conclusions include:

1) Traditional LGES is in essence a frequency-domain modal characteristic, which does not necessarily point to the stability (or oscillation mode) as that of the timedomain eigen-sensitivity. Thus, the derived parameter sensitivity cannot provide an effective guidance for the suppression on the unstable oscillation mode; 2) A complex-valued constant transformation is proposed to link the traditional LGES with the time-domain oscillation mode. In this way, time-domain sensitivity information originally defined on the state space model can now be acquired using the more modularized frequency-domain model, e.g., loop gain model; 3) The established parameter sensitivity contains the information of both magnitude (absolute values) and phase (direction). Its projection on the real axis is more suitable for determining dominant parameters in terms of the oscillation suppression. On the foundation of the established LGES method, its connection with another widely-applied eigen-sensitivity method, i.e., node admittance-based eigen-sensitivity method (NAES), will be further studied in the future work, including their similarities and differences on the physical meaning, oscillation suppression, etc.

figure.head: Fig. 2 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 2. Establishment of the multilayer eigen-sensitivity index system.

(b) and (c). Similarly, Z net,44 , Z net,45 , Z net,54 , Z net,55 , Y con,44 , Y con,45 , Y con,54 and Y con,55 are with large values, meaning that the internal impedance of AC Grid-2 and ac-side of SEC-2 are the root cause of the system oscillation. The sensitivity of their internal parameters is also calculated and shown at the bottom of Fig. 5(b) and (c), respectively. Four parameters L ac g2 ,  cc S1 ,  pll S2 and  cc S2 are with high values, which should be the focus of the parameter tuning for the oscillation suppression.

figure.head: Fig. 4 .
figure.figDesc: Fig. 4. Pole-zero distribution of the oscillation case.
(a) LG-PF matrix (b) Component and parameter sensitivity concerning Z net (c) Component and parameter sensitivity concerning Y con Fig. 5. Results of the multilayer LGES analysis on the test case.

(a) PF (b) Impedance components inside Z net (c) Admittance components inside Y con (e) Parameters of impedance/admittance components inside Z net and Y con Fig. 6. Error Analysis of multilayer LGES indices (the magnitude error between theoretically predicted and numeric obtained sensitivities).
(b) and (c), four parameters L ac g2 ,  cc S1 ,  pll S2 ,

(a) Parameter sensitivity with respect to the oscillation mode (b) Parameter sensitivity with respect to the modal loop gain (c) Time-domain simulations Fig. 7. Parameter sensitivity-based controller tuning.

Z net,44 , Z net,45 , Z net,54 , Z net,55
g2 resistance of AC-grid 2: R ac reactance of AC-grid 2: L ac g2
g3 resistance of AC-grid 3: R ac reactance of AC-grid 3: L ac g3

note.p: con1 Y Y con,11 , Y con,12 , Y con,13 , Y con,21 , Y con,22 , Y con,23 , Y con,31 , Y con,32 , Y con,33control bandwidth:  pll S1 (PLL),  cc S1 (inner current loop),  PQ S1 (power loop) con2 Y control bandwidth:  pll S2 (PLL),  cc S2 (inner current loop),  PQ S2 (power loop) con3 Y net1 Z R dc line resistance: R dc l1 , R dc l2 , R dc l3 dc line reactance: L dc l1 , L dc l2 , L dc l3 Imaginary Axis (seconds -1 )

div.p: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 51837007.
